root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Service Planning Guide
root.summary: Front matter and opening of the Cúram 8.1.2 Service Planning Guide. It states the guide is for business analysts and explains the Cúram Service Planning model, with service plans used to map client needs to goals broken into manageable steps. The section previews the main chapters: overview of service planning, structured planning approach, service plan management, and service/custom service plan items. Key concepts introduced include one goal with sub-goals, plan items, milestones, integrated plans, templates, approvals, contracts, tracking, closure, and client participation. It also notes service and custom service plan items are available only with the Provider Management module and can be updated automatically from CPM delivery/attendance activity. The end of the section contains legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the Notices section on page 41 before using the document or the supported product. It also includes the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: This section is the front matter and start of the Cúram Service Planning Guide. It identifies the edition as applicable to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, then begins the guide’s introduction and overview. It covers purpose, audience, prerequisites, chapter structure, and key concepts of service planning: goals, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, milestones, integrated service plans, templates, contracts, Gantt tracking, ownership, and security/access controls.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section introduces Cúram Service Planning and its administration model: plan participants, service plan types, goals/sub-goals, plan items, service units, costs, approvals, templates, service plan groups, integrated service plans, and management. It explains how administrators configure these elements, how case workers create new or template-based plans, assign participants and ownership, track progress and cost, and handle approvals. It also covers grouped and integrated views for rolled-up costs, milestones, tracking, attachments, and notes.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section covers service plan approval workflows, including plan and plan item approvals, pre/post-approval criteria, milestones and waiver requests, contracts and cloning service plans, event tracking, statuses, outcomes, closure, attachments, communications, notes, tasks, and user roles. It also introduces service and custom service plan items under CPM, explaining configuration, service association, and adding provider constraints. Useful if you need approval rules, lifecycle/status behavior, or CPM-based plan item setup.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-40
children.summary: This section explains how service and custom service plan items are configured and delivered in Cúram Service Planning. It covers authorized units, rates, frequency, renaming, referrals, and nominees; how approvals create service authorizations and line items; and how changes after approval affect them. It also describes automatic updates from CPM for delivery, actual dates, cost, client participation, attendance, and absence records, plus how cancellations and manual actual end dates work. The section ends with a guide summary and related documents.

Front matter and opening chapters of the Cúram Service Planning Guide for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2. It introduces the purpose, audience, prerequisites, and overall service planning model, including goals/sub-goals, plan items, integrated plans, templates, approvals, contracts, tracking, closure, and security. It also covers service/custom service plan items under Provider Management: configuration, approval-generated authorizations, delivery updates from CPM, cost and status tracking, client participation/absence recording, and related guide references.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Contains legal notices: sample data/examples are fictitious, sample programs may be copied/modified/distributed under the Merative Client Agreement for API-conforming development, but are provided “AS IS” without warranty or liability. Points to the Merative privacy policy URL and lists trademarks/registered marks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX, with copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that example names in the document are fictitious, and includes Merative copyright/license and sample-program “AS IS” warranty disclaimer. Provides a privacy policy link. Lists trademarks/registered trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe/PostScript, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, and notes other names may be trademarks of others. Also includes © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

This notices page gives the publication’s legal terms: personal and enterprise reproduction is allowed only with proprietary notices preserved, but distribution, display, derivative works, or external use require Merative consent. It disclaims warranties, export/re-export violations, patent licenses, and responsibility for non-Merative products and external websites. It also notes the document is provided “as-is” and includes copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Service Planning Guide. Lists front matter (Note, Edition) and major chapters/sections: introduction, overview of service planning, structured approach, service plan management, service/custom service plan items, and conclusion. Also includes notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Use this node to navigate the guide’s structure and locate specific topics.

children.title: 1 Cúram Service Planning Guide
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: This section states the guide’s purpose: to provide an overview of Cúram Service Planning so readers understand the service planning model. It is aimed at business analysts with strong knowledge of organizational requirements and basic Social Enterprise Management concepts. It recommends reading the guide in full, plus the Integrated Case Management Guide and Participant Guide first. It also previews the guide’s chapters, including overview, structured approach, and service plan management.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business analysts in the organization, this section states that readers should already understand the organization’s business requirements and basic Social Enterprise Management concepts, especially benefits and services delivery. It also recommends reading the Integrated Case Management Guide and Participant Guide first. This node is useful if you need to confirm the guide’s target audience, prerequisites, and the broader chapter structure of the Cúram Service Planning Guide.

children.title: Prerequisites
children.summary: This section states the guide’s prerequisites: readers should already know basic Social Enterprise Management, especially how benefits and services are delivered to clients. It also recommends reading the Integrated Case Management Guide and Participant Guide first. The section frames the guide as an overview of Cúram Service Planning for business analysts with strong knowledge of organizational business requirements, and lists the main chapters covered in the guide.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This section introduces the Cúram Service Planning Guide, explaining that service plans map benefits or services to individual needs and break goals into manageable steps. It states the guide’s purpose is to provide an overview of the Cúram Service Planning model for business analysts, assuming familiarity with Social Enterprise Management and related guides. It also previews the guide’s chapters: Overview of Service Planning, A Structured Approach to Service Planning, and Service Plan Management.

Introduces the Cúram Service Planning Guide and explains that service plans map benefits or services to client needs and break goals into manageable steps. States the guide’s purpose is to give business analysts an overview of the Cúram Service Planning model. Assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management, especially benefits and services delivery, and recommends reading the Integrated Case Management Guide and Participant Guide first. Also previews the chapters: Overview of Service Planning, A Structured Approach to Service Planning, and Service Plan Management.

children.title: 1.2 Overview of Service Planning
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces Cúram Service Planning and why social enterprise agencies use service plans to help clients achieve goals such as self-sufficiency, return to work, child protection, or permanency. Explains that service plans break a broader goal into steps and that terminology varies by organization and jurisdiction. Notes the U.S. TANF/PRWORA welfare reform context and that the guide uses a Return to Work plan as the running example. Also states the chapter covers service plan requirements and the Cúram features that support them.

children.title: Background
children.summary: Explains the background for service planning in Social Enterprise agencies. It notes that many plan types exist—such as Return to Work, Child Protection, and Permanency plans—and that terminology varies by organization and jurisdiction. It highlights the 1996 PRWORA/Welfare Reform Act, which required TANF families to engage in self-sufficiency activities. The section introduces the sample Return to Work plan used throughout the guide and frames service plans as step-based tools for helping clients achieve specific goals.

children.title: Service Plan Requirements for Social Enterprise Agencies
Provide a Structured Approach
children.summary: Explains why service plans should use a structured, collaborative approach between case worker and client to clarify roles, responsibilities, and best practices. Highlights active client involvement, tracking participation and failures, assigning responsibility for activities, and monitoring actual vs. estimated start/end dates to measure efficiency. Introduces Cúram Service Planning as an integrated, outcome-based framework adaptable across Social Enterprise segments, supporting organization-specific requirements and enabling case workers to build, track, and monitor progress using qualitative and quantitative data.

children.title: Promote Best Practice
children.summary: This section explains how service planning should use a structured, standard approach to promote best practice and reduce training effort. It emphasizes client involvement, collaborative development, clear roles and responsibilities, tracking participation and compliance, and monitoring start/end dates against estimates. It also introduces Cúram Service Planning as an integrated, outcome-based framework that supports diverse social enterprise services and helps case workers track progress and make decisions using qualitative and quantitative data.

children.title: Service Plan Management
children.summary: Introduces service plan management in Cúram Service Planning. Emphasizes a structured, collaborative approach where clients actively help define plans, case workers track participation, responsibilities, and activity dates, and organizations compare actual vs. estimated start/end dates to measure efficiency. Explains that service plans support integrated delivery of services toward a specific client goal across Social Enterprise Management segments, with configurable support for diverse agency needs and an outcome-based model for monitoring progress using qualitative and quantitative data.

Explains that service plans for Social Enterprise agencies are created to help clients achieve a specific goal, usually by breaking that goal into sequential steps. Introduces the chapter’s focus on service plan requirements and Cúram Service Planning, which is designed to meet varied agency needs. Notes that service plans may be named differently across organizations and jurisdictions (for example, Individual Responsibility Plan or Self Sufficiency Plan), and that common plan types include Return to Work, Child Protection, and Permanency plans.

children.title: Cúram Service Planning
Goal
children.summary: Defines the service plan goal as the primary objective or desired outcome the client works toward to become independent of the organization. States each service plan must have one overall goal. Also introduces related service plan elements: sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, and milestones, which are used to break down and track progress toward the goal.

children.title: Sub-Goal
children.summary: This section explains sub-goals in Cúram service plans. A service plan has one overall goal, which can be divided into sub-goals, and sub-goals may be grouped into plan groups for easier organization. It sits within a broader overview of service plan elements, including plan items and milestones, which support tracking outcomes and significant events. Open this node if you need the definition of sub-goals or how they relate to goals, plan groups, and the overall service plan structure.

children.title: Plan Groups
children.summary: Plan Groups explains that service plans can contain one overall goal, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, and milestones. Plan groups are user-created containers used to logically organize related sub-goals, plan items, and existing plan groups within the Service Plan Tree Structure, and they can be nested. The section also situates plan groups within the broader service planning model and notes that plan items and milestones track progress toward the plan goal.

children.title: Plan Item
children.summary: Plan items are the fundamental building blocks of a service plan. They represent benefits, services, and activities added to achieve the plan goal, each with a desired outcome such as employment or course completion. Plan items can be broken into service units, which are required tasks measured by a recommended number of units. The section also notes that plan items are customizable to fit different social enterprise requirements and can be incorporated into the service plan model.

children.title: Milestones
children.summary: Milestones in Service Planning track significant events or tasks during a service plan’s life cycle, such as plan item progress. Each milestone has expected and actual start/end dates, and ownership can be assigned to someone other than the service plan owner. Custom workflow can trigger alerts when milestones start late or run past their expected end. Milestones may be created manually or automatically from service plan events.

children.title: Integrated Service Plans
children.summary: Introduces integrated service plans as a rolled-up case-level view that summarizes all service plan groups and plans, including milestones, costs, goals, and progress. Explains service plan ownership: the case owner is set as initial owner by default, ownership can be transferred, and owners may be users or org groups (unit/position/work queue). Also notes key features like service plan templates and service plan contracts, which support standardized creation and formal client/agency commitment.

children.title: Service Plan Ownership
children.summary: This section explains service plan ownership in Cúram Service Planning. By default, the case owner becomes the initial owner of a new service plan, but ownership can be transferred and assigned to a user or an संगठन/group such as an organization unit, position, or work queue, allowing members to work on it. It also notes that agencies can configure their own ownership strategy. The page briefly continues into additional features like service plan templates and contracts.

children.title: Additional Features
Service Plan Template
children.summary: This section explains service plan templates as predefined plans containing goals, sub-goals, plan items, and milestones. It notes that workers can start from a template instead of building a plan from scratch, improving consistency, best practice, and training for novice case workers, while still allowing customization for individual clients. The surrounding text also covers automatic milestone creation, integrated service plan views, and service plan ownership options.

children.title: Service Plan Contract
children.summary: This section describes service plan features in Cúram, including automatic milestone creation, integrated service plan views, and service plan ownership rules. It also covers service plan templates for reusable, customizable plans. The Service Plan Contract part explains that once the agency and client agree the plan meets the client’s needs, a contract is issued to formalize commitment to the planned activities.

children.title: Service Plan Gantt Chart
children.summary: This section describes the Service Plan Gantt chart in Cúram Service Planning. It explains that the Tracking Gantt gives a single-page, expandable tree view of service plan artifacts: goals, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, and milestones. It also introduces service plan security, covering function security, field security, location-based security (On, Off, Restricted View, Read Only), and sensitivity controls for plan elements and notes.

children.title: Service Plan Security
children.summary: Explains Service Plan Security in Cúram Service Planning. Covers how plans are protected from unauthorized access through function security, field security, location-based security, and sensitivity controls. Notes that users may be restricted from viewing, maintaining, approving, or cloning service plans and plan items. Describes location security modes: Off, On, Restricted View, and Read Only, plus exceptions via extra location rights. Also states that contracts, participants, sub-goals, plan items, and notes can have sensitivity levels controlling access.

children.title: Plan Participants
children.summary: This section explains plan participants in a service plan: primary plan participant, additional plan participants, nominated representatives, and correspondents. It describes what the plan participants list shows (name, type, start/end dates, status) and how participants are added, removed, end-dated, or deleted. It also notes that the primary participant is created from a case member, while nominated representatives and correspondents are added separately, with different rules for end dating and deletion.

This section introduces additional service planning features: predefined service plan templates for faster, more consistent plan creation; service plan contracts that formalize agreement and can be viewed/printed; an expandable Tracking Gantt chart showing goals through milestones; and security controls. Security covers function and field permissions, location-based access modes (On, Off, Restricted View, Read Only), and sensitivity levels for plan elements and notes.

This section introduces Cúram Service Planning as a structured, outcome-based way to plan and monitor services for a client. It defines the core elements: one overall goal, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, and milestones, including service units and milestone timing/automation. It also covers integrated service plan views, service plan ownership, templates, contracts, a Tracking Gantt chart, and security controls such as function, field, location-based, and sensitivity access.

Introduces service planning for Social Enterprise agencies: why organizations use service plans, common plan types (Return to Work, Child Protection, Permanency), and the PRWORA/TANF welfare reform context. Explains that service plans break a goal into structured steps, often with collaborative client/case worker involvement. Describes Cúram Service Planning as an outcome-based framework supporting goals, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, milestones, integrated views, ownership, templates, contracts, Gantt tracking, and security controls.

children.title: 1.3 A Structured Approach to Service Planning
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces Cúram Service Planning and the concept of plan participants, including primary plan participant, additional participants, nominated representatives, and correspondents, with their list details and lifecycle rules. Also outlines the structured approach to service planning: the Service Planning Administration and Application components, what administrators can configure (goals, sub-goals, plan items, milestones, expected outcomes, good cause, contract text, templates), and the first setup step of defining service plan types such as Return to Work or Child Welfare.

children.title: Administering a Service Plan
Service Plan Type
children.summary: This section introduces service plan types as the first step in configuring a service plan. It explains that organizations define types such as Return to Work or Child Welfare within the Service Planning Administration component. The surrounding context describes plan participants, including primary participants, nominated representatives, and correspondents, and notes how they are added, removed, or end-dated. It also frames service planning as a structured approach with administration and application components, supporting goals, plan items, milestones, outcomes, and templates.

children.title: Goal
children.summary: Explains how service plan administration defines goals, sub-goals, and plan items. A goal is the main client objective, and one or more goals can be linked to a service plan type for selection during plan creation. Goals can be broken into categorized sub-goals, such as Transportation or Childcare, and each sub-goal can have contract text and one or more linked plan items. This supports structured planning toward client independence and goal achievement.

children.title: Sub-Goal
children.summary: Explains how service plan sub-goals are configured and used. Administrators can link one or more sub-goals to a service plan goal, with sub-goals grouped by type and selected by case workers when creating plans. Examples include Transportation, Childcare, and Find a Job for a Sustainable Employment goal. The section also notes that contract text can be defined for each goal and sub-goal, displayed in the participant’s preferred language, and that sub-goals can be tied to one or more plan items required to achieve them.

children.title: Goal and Sub-Goal Contract Text
children.summary: This section explains how service plan goals and sub-goals can have contract text defined in administration. When a service plan contract is generated, the goal and sub-goal text is shown in the participant’s or representative’s preferred language. It sits within broader service plan setup, where administrators can define goals, sub-goals, approval checks, contract generation rules, integrated case links, milestones, and plan items.

children.title: Plan Item
Concerning Participant
children.summary: This section explains the “concerning participant” in a service plan: the participant whose need the plan item addresses. Case workers must choose one from the plan participants, and every plan item must have one before it can be saved or the service plan approved. It also briefly relates this field to responsible participant assignment, configurable plan item requirements, service units, and plan item cost, showing how service plans can be customized and tracked.

children.title: Responsible Participant
children.summary: This section explains the “responsible participant” for a service plan item: the participant or user assigned by the case worker to ensure the item is completed. It also places this in the broader plan item configuration context, noting related settings such as concerning participant, cost, approval options, expected outcome, name modification, good-cause reasons, attachments, service units, and custom plan item behavior. Useful for understanding who owns plan item completion and how plan items are configured in Cúram Service Planning.

children.title: Diverse Plan Item Requirements
children.summary: This section explains how Service Planning supports diverse, custom plan item requirements. It covers configurable plan item attributes such as concerning and responsible participants, cost, approval options, expected outcome, name editability, good causes for unmet outcomes, and attachments. It also describes custom plan item types, integration with application objects, and custom delivery pages. Additional topics include service units and authorized quantities, plus estimated vs. actual plan item cost.

children.title: Service Units
children.summary: Explains configurable service plan item details and introduces service units. Plan items can define concerning and responsible participants, cost, approval options, expected outcomes, name editability, good causes, and attachments. The service units subsection describes measurable delivery elements (e.g., visits or training sessions), with a service unit type indicating whether a plan is based on units or duration. Authorized users set recommended units, which can later be increased up to a maximum during delivery.

children.title: Plan Item Cost
children.summary: Plan item cost can store both estimated and actual costs for a plan item. Estimated cost is the expected amount an organization plans to pay for delivering the associated service, while actual cost is the amount actually paid to deliver that service.

children.title: Plan Item Approval
children.summary: This section describes plan item configuration in Cúram Service Planning Administration. It covers approval options, including whether approval is required, pre- and post-approval criteria, workflow processes, and priority handling for multiple criteria. It also explains task configuration for plan items, expected outcomes (mandatory for each plan item), optional good cause reasons, attachments, and Guidance URLs for supporting information.

children.title: Plan Item Task Configuration
children.summary: This section explains plan item task configuration in Cúram Service Planning Administration. It covers how to define task name, subject, priority, allocation or reservation to the plan item owner, and the action taken when addressing the task. It also notes related plan item setup topics on the page: approval criteria, expected outcomes, good cause, attachments, and guidance URLs. Use this node if you need details on configuring tasks or surrounding plan item configuration options.

children.title: Expected Outcome
children.summary: Defines expected outcomes for plan items in Cúram Service Planning. An expected outcome is the desired result of an activity (for example, a Full Time Job Search aiming for Full Time Employment) and at least one must be configured for every plan item under the outcome-based approach. The section also notes related configuration items such as good cause, attachments, and a Guidance URL, though those are described separately.

children.title: Good Cause
children.summary: Explains plan item configuration in Cúram Service Planning Administration, focusing on approval options, task setup, expected outcomes, good cause, attachments, and guidance URLs. Good cause is an optional reason a participant did not achieve the expected outcome (e.g., certified illness) and may matter when sanctions apply. The section notes that good cause is not mandatory for plan item configuration.

children.title: Attachments
children.summary: This section notes that case workers can maintain attachments for a plan item, but refers readers to the Integrated Case Management Guide for details. It also covers related plan item configuration topics: approval criteria and workflows, task configuration, expected outcomes, good cause, and Guidance URLs.

children.title: Guidance URL
children.summary: This section explains that a Guidance URL can be configured for a plan item in Cúram Service Planning Administration by entering a web address to provide supporting guidance. It appears alongside other plan item setup options such as approval criteria, task configuration, expected outcomes, good cause, and attachments. Use this section if you need the specific configuration point for linking external guidance to a plan item.

This section explains how sub-goals link to one or more plan items in a service plan and how plan items are configured. It covers customizable plan item requirements, including concerning participant, responsible participant, estimated/actual cost, approval options, expected outcome, name editability, good cause, attachments, service units, and Guidance URL. It also describes approval and task configuration, plus how plan items can be tied to application objects and custom delivery pages. Useful if you need core plan item setup or delivery behavior details.

children.title: Milestone Configurations
children.summary: This section explains milestone configuration settings for service plan milestones. It covers mandatory fields like Name and Type, Earliest Start Day, Duration, and Start Date, plus optional End Date and Expected Date Extension Allowed. It describes how these settings determine expected start/end dates for manual and automatic milestones, how validation works, and how configuration active periods are controlled.

children.title: Associating Milestones with Service Plans
children.summary: Explains how to associate milestone configurations with a service plan type to support manual or automatic milestone creation. You can either create a new milestone configuration during the association or select an existing one. For automatic milestones, key settings are creation and completion events, which trigger instance creation and closure. The section gives an example using Approve Plan and Close Plan events to track a service plan lifecycle from approval to closure.

children.title: Service Plan Template
children.summary: Defines service plan templates as predefined sub-goals, plan items, and milestones used to support consistent service delivery and adapt to legislative changes. Explains template hierarchy, mandatory plan items, override of approval requirements, and reuse/modification of approval criteria. Notes templates must be linked to service plan types to be available when creating plans. Also introduces service plan groups in integrated plans and how service plan types are assigned to them. Ends with service plan ownership strategy, which can use workflow to determine initial ownership by plan type.

children.title: Service Plan Group
children.summary: This section explains how service plan groups are configured in Cúram Service Planning Administration. It shows that organizations add defined service plan types, or create new types, and associate them to a group to control which service plans can be included in an integrated service plan. It also situates group configuration alongside related topics on service plan templates and ownership strategy. Open this node if you need setup details for grouping service plan types and determining allowable plans within an integrated case plan.

children.title: Configuring Service Plan Ownership
children.summary: Describes the Service Plan Ownership Strategy setting, which lets administrators define workflow-based ownership for a service plan type. It determines how the initial case owner for a service plan is assigned and functions similarly to case ownership. Useful for organizations that need to override the default service plan ownership behavior.

Covers service plan administration setup: defining service plan types and their approval checks, contract generation, integrated case links, and milestones. Explains configuring goals, sub-goals, contract text, and plan items, including participants, costs, approvals, tasks, outcomes, service units, good cause, attachments, and guidance URLs. Also describes milestone configurations and associating them to plan types, plus templates, service plan groups, and workflow-based ownership strategy. Open for broad setup of service plan structure and behavior.

children.title: Creating a Service Plan
A New Service Plan
Service Plan Cost
children.summary: Explains how service plan cost is calculated as the sum of individual service plan item costs. Introduces that plan item costs may be derived in multiple ways, with more details continuing beyond the excerpt. Useful if you need how Cúram determines overall service plan cost from item-level costing.

Describes how a case worker creates a new service plan versus using a template, and how plans can include multiple case members, with a primary plan participant and additional participants. Explains required elements of a new plan: goal, sub-goals, plan items, optional milestones, and plan groups. Introduces Basic Plan Items and Custom Basic Plan Items, plus Service Unit Delivery for tracking progress. Notes that plan item ownership can be assigned to users, organization units, or participants. Also begins the Service Plan Cost topic, stating total cost is the sum of item costs.

children.title: Service Plan Template
children.summary: Explains how service plan templates predefine plan items, milestones, expected dates, outcomes, authorized units, and approval criteria based on assessed client needs, while allowing case workers to add or remove non-mandatory elements. Also describes integrated service plans as a rolled-up view across service plan groups and standalone plans, summarizing costs, goals/progress via a tracking Gantt, and milestones.

Explains how a case worker creates a service plan either from scratch or from a predefined template, and that plans can include multiple case members with a primary and additional participants. A new plan must define a goal, sub-goals, and plan items, with optional milestones and plan groups. Introduces Basic Plan Items, Custom Basic Plan Items, and Service Unit Delivery for tracking progress, plus ownership assignment for plan items. Begins cost calculation, noting total service plan cost is the sum of item costs.

children.title: Integrated Service Plans
Service Plan Groups
children.summary: Defines service plan groups within an integrated service plan as a rolled-up view of costs, goals, milestones, and progress for related service plans. Explains that service plans can be added either to a group or directly to the integrated plan, with allowable plan types configured in administration. Notes users can close all plans in a group at once. Lists group-level features: costs, tracking Gantt, attachments, notes, and milestones.

Integrated service plans provide a case-wide summary of all service plan groups and standalone service plans, rolling up milestones, costs, goals, and progress. A plan can include one or more groups, each containing one or more service plans, but plans may also exist independently. At this level, users can view overall costs, a tracking Gantt showing goals/sub-goals/plan items/progress, and a consolidated milestones list.

Cúram Service Planning provides a structured platform with Administration and Application components. Administrators define service plan types, goals, sub-goals, plan items, milestones, templates, contract text, approval checks, service units, good cause, and ownership/workflow behavior. Case workers create plans from scratch or templates, assign primary/additional participants, and track costs, outcomes, and progress through basic/custom plan items and service unit delivery. Integrated service plans roll up multiple plans/groups, showing consolidated costs, tracking Gantt, and milestones.

children.title: 1.4 Service Plan Management
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces service plan management and related approval features in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains service plan groups within an integrated service plan, which provide rolled-up views of costs, goals, milestones, progress, attachments, notes, and allow closing all plans in the group at once. It then outlines the service plan approval process: plans are submitted after case worker and client agree, can be approved at the whole-plan or item level, and submitted milestone waiver requests can also be approved.

children.title: Approval Process
Service Plan Approval
children.summary: Explains the service plan approval step in Service Plan Management. A service plan is typically submitted by the case worker after the plan is defined; users with approval privileges trigger automatic approval, otherwise manual approval is required. The broader approval process ensures the plan’s services and activities are appropriate and cost effective, and Cúram can approve the full plan and its components, including individual plan items and submitted milestone waiver requests.

children.title: Plan Item Approval
children.summary: Explains plan item approval within service plans. Plan items may be auto-approved if approval is not required, or if submitted by users with approval privileges; otherwise they need manual approval. Pre-approval criteria must be satisfied before approval, potentially via prioritized workflow tasks, and failure returns the item to unapproved status. Post-approval criteria trigger workflow after approval for downstream business actions. Approval criteria come from the service plan template for template-based items, or from the item itself when created manually.

children.title: Milestone Waiver Request Approval
children.summary: Explains the milestone waiver request approval process for changing expected start/end dates on automatically created milestones. Covers when a waiver is needed, including settings that allow date extensions and require waivers, and notes manually created milestones can be changed without a request. Describes approval-check hierarchy across user, organization unit, and milestone levels, the percentage-based approval logic, submission/status flow, notifications, and that only one waiver per milestone can be submitted at a time.

Covers service plan approval in Service Plan Management, including automatic vs manual approval based on submitter privileges, approval request recording, baselines, and approval of unapproved plan items during plan approval. Explains individual plan item approval, including pre/post-approval criteria, workflow tasks, template-based vs manual criteria, and task creation on approval. Also describes milestone waiver request approval for changing expected dates on automatically created milestones, including settings that allow/require waivers, approval-check precedence across user/organization/milestone levels, status flow, notifications, and one submitted waiver per milestone.

children.title: Contracts
children.summary: Defines service plan contracts as formal agreements between client(s) and the organization, created collaboratively and issued to participants or nominated representatives in the participant’s preferred language. Explains acceptance/rejection workflow, signatories, and how acceptance activates the approved plan. Notes that after issuance some plan changes are restricted, but tracking details like milestones, baselines, participation, and notes can still change. Covers printing/previewing contracts, multiple-contract configurations, and cloning active or closed service plans to create a new open version with copied groups, items, milestones, and attachments.

children.title: Cloning Service Plans
children.summary: Explains service plan cloning as a way to create a new version of an existing plan when a client’s needs change. A cloned plan starts in Open status and preserves the original plan groups, sub-goals, plan items, milestones, and any attachments. Cloning works for both active and closed service plans, and the cloned plan can then be modified and reissued as a new contract. Useful for understanding how Cúram supports plan versioning when multiple contracts are not allowed.

children.title: Tracking Service Plan Progress
Tracking Gantt Chart
children.summary: Describes Cúram Service Planning’s Tracking Gantt Chart for monitoring service plan progress against expectations. Explains that the chart shows an expandable tree of goals, sub-goals, plan items, and milestones with hyperlinks to detail pages, and can display actual, expected, or combined time periods. It also shows summary data such as start/end dates and duration, and can display participant names for plan items. Includes an example “Return to Work” chart and notes the Show All option and tooltip summaries.

children.title: Baselines
children.summary: This section covers service plan baselines, costs, client participation, and milestone tracking. It explains that baselines are snapshots of a plan at a point in time, viewable in list or Gantt format, and can be created automatically on submit/approve or manually. It also describes how plan item costs may be fixed, actual, or calculated from product delivery, and how cost statements compare estimated vs. actual costs. Client participation tracking includes attendance, absence, progress, and weekly totals. Milestones help monitor key lifecycle events and delays.

children.title: Cost
children.summary: This section explains cost tracking in Cúram Service Planning. Plan item cost can come from a fixed configured cost, actual delivery cost entered by a case worker, or a running total from related product delivery. It also describes cost statements, which can be generated at any time to show estimated and actual costs for plan items, sub-goals, plan groups, and the overall plan in a tree structure.

children.title: Client Participation
children.summary: This section explains client participation tracking in Cúram Service Planning. Case workers can record daily, partial, or non-attendance for plan items, along with time participated, time absent, reasons for absence, and progress reasons. Attendance can be tracked for in-progress items and items the client is responsible for, helping assess commitment, compliance, and whether sanctions may be needed. Weekly participation totals are also calculated and displayed.

children.title: Milestone Tracking
children.summary: Milestone tracking consolidates features for monitoring key events in a service plan’s lifecycle. Milestones define important life events, associate expected time frames, and help agencies detect delays. When milestones slip, additional processing can be triggered to recover schedule alignment.

children.title: Events
children.summary: This section explains service plan events and statuses in Cúram Service Planning. It lists major tracked events—Plan Opened, Submitted, Approved, Rejected, Cloned, Contract Printed, Milestone Added, and Closed—and notes they can be viewed in a calendar or list. It also distinguishes service plan status, contract status, and plan item status. Contracts start as Issued and can be Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled; acceptance makes the service plan Active.

Cúram Service Planning tracks service plan progress through a Gantt chart, baselines, cost statements, client participation, milestones, and events. The Gantt chart shows goals through milestones in an expandable tree with links, expected vs. actual timing, summary dates/duration, and configurable participant names. Baselines capture plan snapshots automatically or manually. Cost statements compare estimated and actual costs. Client participation records attendance, absence, progress, and weekly totals. Milestones monitor key lifecycle events and delays.

children.title: Plan Status
Contract and Service Plan Status
children.summary: Explains service plan and contract status behavior. Milestones have expected and actual start/end dates, with possible lapse detection and workflow triggers for delays. Lists key service plan events such as opened, submitted, approved, rejected, cloned, contract printed, milestone added, and closed. Describes three status types: service plan, contract, and plan item. A new contract starts as Issued, then may be Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled; only acceptance changes the service plan status to Active.

children.title: Plan Item Status
children.summary: Explains the lifecycle statuses for a plan item in Cúram Service Planning. Plan items may be Unapproved, Submitted, Not Started, In Progress, or Completed, depending on approval, activation, and recorded start/end dates. It distinguishes approval-required items from those that skip approval, and notes that “In Progress” means the item is activated. The section also briefly follows into service plan outcomes, describing expected and actual outcomes used to determine whether plan goals are attained.

Describes service plan status concepts and lifecycle tracking. Milestones have expected and actual start/end dates, with lapse detection and optional workflow actions for delays. Lists key plan events: opened, submitted, approved, rejected, cloned, contract printed, milestone added, and closed. Explains three status types: service plan, contract, and plan item. A new contract starts as Issued and may be Accepted, Rejected, or Canceled; only acceptance changes the service plan to Active.

children.title: Service Plan Outcomes
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Service Planning records outcomes for service plans and plan items. It describes the outcome workflow: each plan item starts with an expected outcome, then an actual outcome of Attained, Not Attained, or Canceled is recorded on completion. After all plan items in a sub-goal are completed, the sub-goal outcome can be entered. The overall service plan outcome is recorded after all sub-goal outcomes are entered, and the organization defines what counts as the plan’s goal being attained.

children.title: Service Plan Closure
children.summary: Explains when a service plan can be closed and what happens afterward: it is usually closed after all activities finish, but can be closed anytime during delivery, and remains accessible from the integrated case. On closure, a customizable communication may be generated for plan participants. Also outlines additional service plan features modeled on core functionality, including attachments (e.g., scanned documents), communications, and notes with priority, sensitivity, edit/append history, and user access controls.

children.title: Additional Features
Attachments
children.summary: This section describes additional service plan features, focusing on attachments. Attachments are supplemental files added to a service plan, such as scanned documents, signed contracts, Word/Excel files, and PDFs, and are accessible to users with appropriate security. It also briefly covers related features: service plan closure and optional closure communications, communications linked to the plan, and notes with priority, sensitivity, editing, appending, and history tracking.

children.title: Communications
children.summary: Covers communications in service plans, including creation of communications from a service plan communication list, automatic linkage to the plan, supported channels (paper, phone, email), and integration with Word/XSL templates or email servers. Also notes that communications may be recorded for the participant or a nominated representative. This section sits within additional service plan features and is useful if you need how plan-related correspondence is handled.

children.title: Notes
children.summary: This section explains service plan notes and related features. Notes are free-text entries that can be prioritized and assigned a sensitivity rating to restrict access. Users can update note priority and sensitivity, append additional text, and sometimes edit the original text within a configured time window. The system keeps a note history with each version, timestamps, and the modifying user. It also briefly covers service plan closure, attachments, and communications.

children.title: Tasks
children.summary: This section explains service plan closure and related features. It says a service plan is usually closed when activities are complete, but may be closed anytime; closed plans remain accessible from the integrated case. Closure can generate a communication for plan participants, customizable with outcomes and outstanding issues. It also covers additional service plan features: attachments (various file types and shared access by privileged users), communications (paper, phone, email, templates), and notes (free text, priority, sensitivity, editing, and history tracking).

children.title: User Roles
children.summary: Defines service plan user roles: owner and supervisor. The owner is responsible for the service plan, and the supervisor manages it; both can be viewed or maintained at any time. The section also begins the topic of service and custom service plan items, explaining that when CPM is implemented, case workers can add these items to plans, associate them with services, and optionally specify provider type or provider at the sub-goal level.

This section describes additional service plan features: attachments, communications, and notes. Attachments can store supplemental files such as scanned documents, contracts, Word/Excel files, and PDFs, with access controlled by security privileges. Communications created from a service plan are automatically linked to it and can use paper, phone, email, templates, or email servers. Notes are free-text entries with priority and sensitivity settings, editable/appended under configuration, and full history tracking. It also notes plan closure and optional closure communications.

Cúram Service Plan Management covers how plans are approved, tracked, versioned, and closed. It explains service plan groups with rolled-up costs, Gantt tracking, attachments, notes, and milestones; approval workflows for whole plans, individual plan items, and milestone waiver requests; contract issuance, acceptance, rejection, and cloning of active/closed plans; tracking via baselines, costs, participation, milestones, events, statuses, outcomes, and closure communications; plus attachments, communications, and notes features.

children.title: 1.5 Service and Custom Service Plan Items
Introduction
children.summary: This section introduces service and custom service plan items in Cúram Service Planning when CPM is implemented. It explains that providers registered in CPM can be included in service plans, and case workers can add these items and record participant participation. It distinguishes configured service plan items, which are tied to a specific CPM service by an administrator, from custom service plan items, which select the service when added by a case worker. It also notes optional provider type or provider selection at the sub-goal level.

children.title: Configuring Service and Custom Service Plan Items
children.summary: Explains how service plan items and custom service plan items are configured in Cúram Service Planning when CPM is implemented. Standard service plan items are tied to a specific CPM service during admin configuration, while custom service plan items have no service selected until a case worker adds them to a plan. Both derive schedule, cost, status, and units delivered from CPM invoice processing and attendance tracking. Also notes that, when adding either item to a sub-goal, a provider type or specific provider may be optionally specified.

children.title: Adding Service and Custom Service Plan Items to a Sub-goal
Setting the Rate and Units Authorized
children.summary: Explains how a case worker sets service plan item authorization details: required units authorized, optional authorized rate, and how these appear in the service plan contract. It also notes that delivered units update automatically via invoice processing or attendance tracking and cannot be edited by the case worker. The section covers optional frequency setup for service delivery dates, approval-related limits on changing frequency/start/end dates, and the ability to rename plan items for clarity in the Tracking Gantt chart.

children.title: Setting the Frequency
children.summary: This section explains how case workers set the frequency for service plan items or custom service plan items. Frequency defines when the service is delivered, such as weekly on specific days, and together with expected start and end dates determines authorized service dates. If approval is required, frequency can be changed until approval; otherwise it must be set at creation. The frequency is shown on the Tracking Gantt chart.

children.title: Renaming Service and Custom Service Plan Items
children.summary: This section explains that case workers can rename service and custom service plan items to make them easier to distinguish and better reflect a participant’s needs. It notes related plan-item setup options such as specifying authorized units, optional rate, frequency, referral details, and nominee/payee information. It also mentions that these details appear in the service plan contract and Tracking Gantt chart, helping identify and track plan items.

This section explains how case workers add service and custom service plan items to a sub-goal and what can be configured: provider or provider type, required units authorized, optional authorized rate, optional frequency, referral details, and nominee/payee. It describes how authorized and delivered units are calculated and updated through CPM invoice processing or attendance tracking, and how these details appear in the service plan contract and Tracking Gantt chart. It also covers when frequency can be changed and the option to rename plan items.

children.title: Service and Custom Service Plan Item Delivery
Approving a Service Plan Item that does not have a Frequency Specified
children.summary: Explains how approving a service or custom service plan item creates service authorizations and line items. If no frequency is specified, approval creates one line item covering the expected start and end dates, with authorized units and rate taken from the plan item. Gives an example using Jan 1–11, 2008, 2 units, and $200. Also contrasts this with frequency-based approval, where multiple line items are created per scheduled dates and total authorized units are calculated from them.

children.title: Approving a Plan Item that has a Frequency Specified
children.summary: Explains how approving a service/custom service plan item with a specified frequency creates one service authorization and multiple authorization line items. The number of line items is based on the frequency plus expected start and end dates, with each line item representing one authorized service day. Total units authorized are calculated from units per line item multiplied by the number of generated line items. Includes an example where weekly Tuesday/Wednesday frequency over a date range produces 4 line items and 8 total units.

children.title: Making Changes after Approval
children.summary: Explains how approved service plan items can be changed or canceled and how those changes flow to related service authorization line items. For non-frequency items, updating dates, units, or rate updates the authorization line item; for frequency-based items, only units or rate can change, not dates, and all related authorization line items are updated. Also describes automatic updates when service units are delivered: actual start/end dates, status, units delivered, and actual cost are populated from invoices or attendance rosters.

children.title: Recording Service and Custom Service Plan Item Details
children.summary: Explains how approved service plan items are updated after service delivery or later changes. Covers post-approval edits to expected dates, units, and rates, cancellation behavior, and how delivered units from invoices or attendance rosters automatically update the plan item’s actual start/end dates, status, units delivered, and actual cost. Useful for understanding service authorization updates, completion tracking, and how custom plan item details are recorded in CPM/Cúram.

Explains how approved service and custom service plan items create service authorizations and line items, with behavior differing by frequency. Without a frequency, approval creates one line item spanning expected start/end dates. With a frequency, approval generates multiple dated line items and calculates total authorized units from them. Covers post-approval edits and cancellations: non-frequency items can update dates/units/rate; frequency-based items only units/rate, with all line items updated or canceled. Also describes automatic updates from delivered services (actual dates, status, units delivered, actual cost).

children.title: Client Participation
Updating Client Progress for a Plan Item
children.summary: Covers how case workers update a plan item’s client progress. Client participation for service and custom service plan items can be created manually or automatically from CPM-delivered services, but automatically created participation cannot be modified. The exception is daily progress updates, where workers can note whether the client is making progress and add details. Also notes that actual end dates may be manually entered to complete a plan item, triggering closure or cancellation of related authorization line items.

children.title: Client Absence Information
children.summary: Describes how client absence information is captured for service and custom service plan items. If CPM is not reporting daily attendance, absence periods from roster line items are automatically recorded on the plan item. Case workers can also enter absences manually when recording client participation, but they cannot edit absences created automatically. This section sits alongside client participation and progress tracking for plan items and explains how absence data is populated through CPM integration.

Describes client participation for service and custom service plan items. Participation can be created manually by case workers or automatically from CPM-delivered services via roster or invoice line items; automatically created participation cannot be edited. The exception is daily progress updates, where workers can record whether the client is making progress and add details. The section also covers client absence capture: absences may be automatically recorded from CPM roster information when daily attendance is not reported, or entered manually, but automatic absences cannot be modified.

Explains how Cúram Service Planning uses CPM-managed providers to add service and custom service plan items to a service plan. Covers configuration differences (service fixed by admin vs selected by case worker), optional provider/provider type, units authorized, rate, frequency, referral/nominee, and renaming. Describes approval behavior: creation of service authorizations and line items with or without frequency, and how dates, units, and rates determine them. Also details automatic updates from CPM invoice/attendance processing, including actual dates, status, units delivered, and actual cost.

children.title: 1.6 Conclusion
Summary
children.summary: This section summarizes the guide’s main points: Cúram Service Planning supports social enterprise agencies with structured service plans for clients, typically organized around one goal, sub-goals, and plan items. It also highlights management tools such as Tracking Gantt, Baselines, Cost Statement, and Client Participation for monitoring progress and outcomes. The section notes that service and custom plan items can be configured and that client participation can be recorded and measured. It also points readers to related Cúram guides for further background.

children.title: Additional Information
children.summary: This section is a closing “Additional Information” and legal notice appendix for the Cúram Service Planning Guide. It lists related guides (Participant, Integrated Case Management, Issue Management, Evidence, Financials, Workflow, Communications, and Customizing Plan Items) and then provides licensing, copyright, disclaimer, privacy policy, and trademark notices. It is mainly useful for identifying related documentation and the document’s usage/reuse terms, not for service planning functionality.

This conclusion summarizes Cúram Service Planning: it supports social enterprise agencies with structured service plans, typically one goal with sub-goals and plan items, and provides monitoring tools like Tracking Gantt, Baselines, Cost Statement, and Client Participation. It notes that service and custom service plan items can be configured and that client participation and progress can be recorded and measured. The section also lists related Cúram guides and then shifts to legal notices, licensing terms, privacy policy, trademarks, and copyright/sample code disclaimers.

Introductory section to the Cúram Service Planning Guide for business analysts familiar with SEM, benefits, and services. Explains that service plans map client needs to a goal broken into manageable steps, and previews the guide’s main chapters. Introduces common plan types and the structured Cúram model: one goal, sub-goals, plan groups, plan items, milestones, collaborative ownership, tracking, approvals, templates, and integrated plans. Later pages also cover service/custom plan items using Provider Management and conclude with related guides plus legal notices.
